name: Nut Extended
description: Nut, in Egyptian mythology, is the goddess of the sky and heavens. 'Nut Extended' refers to the common artistic depiction of Nut as an arch stretching over the earth, her body adorned with stars. She is the daughter of Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture), and the mother of Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and in some accounts, Horus the Elder. Nut's primary role is to swallow the sun god Ra each evening and give birth to him again each morning, thus representing the cycle of day and night. Her body is often depicted supported by Shu and Geb (earth), separating the sky from the earth. The story of her forced separation from Geb, ordered by Ra due to a prophecy that her children would overthrow him, is a central myth. Thoth, through a game of dice with the moon, won five extra days of the year, allowing Nut to give birth to her five children outside of the traditionally recognized 360 days, circumventing Ra's curse. As the sky goddess, Nut holds dominion over the celestial realm, stars, and the movement of heavenly bodies. She represents the vastness of the cosmos and is associated with protection, motherhood, and rebirth. Her extended form is a visual representation of the cosmos itself, encapsulating and protecting the world below.
subtitle: Sky Goddess, Mother of Stars

keyMyths.title: The Birth of the Gods
keyMyths.description: Nut, cursed by Ra not to give birth on any day of the year, was aided by Thoth, who won five extra days, allowing her to birth Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and Horus the Elder.

keyMyths.title: Separation of Sky and Earth
keyMyths.description: Ra, fearing a prophecy, ordered Shu to separate Nut (sky) from Geb (earth), creating the space between them and allowing light to exist.

keyMyths.title: The Swallowing of Ra
keyMyths.description: Each evening, Nut swallows Ra, the sun god, and gives birth to him again in the morning, representing the cycle of day and night and rebirth.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
sources.description: Detailed account of Egyptian religious beliefs.
sources.text: Detailed account of Egyptian religious beliefs.

sources.author: Geraldine Pinch
sources.work: Egyptian Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Goddesses, and Traditions of Ancient Egypt

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Food and Drink
worshipPractices.description: Nut, as a primordial goddess, received offerings of food and drink to ensure the continued cycle of day and night and the fertility of the land. These offerings were often left at temples dedicated to broader cosmic deities or at household shrines.

worshipPractices.name: Amulets and Protective Spells
worshipPractices.description: Amulets depicting Nut were worn for protection, particularly during childbirth. Spells invoking her name were also recited to safeguard against evil spirits and ensure a safe passage into the afterlife, associating her with protection and cosmic order.

festivals.name: Birthdays of Osiris, Isis, Set, and Nephthys
festivals.description: While not directly a festival *for* Nut, the celebrations of her children (Osiris, Isis, Set, and Nephthys) indirectly honored her role as their mother. These birthdays were significant religious events with processions, offerings, and reenactments of mythological events.

festivals.name: New Year Festival
festivals.description: As a sky goddess intrinsically linked to the passage of time and the cosmos, Nut was likely honored during the New Year festival. The festival celebrated renewal, rebirth, and the cyclical nature of life, all concepts tied to Nut's swallowing and rebirth of the sun.

cultural.modernLegacy: Nut's image as the arched sky goddess continues to inspire art, literature, and modern interpretations of Egyptian mythology. She symbolizes the vastness of the cosmos and the cyclical nature of time, influencing contemporary spiritual and artistic expression.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In the beginning, according to Egyptian cosmology, there was only Nun, the primordial waters of chaos. From Nun emerged Atum, the self-created deity, who then created Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture). Nut was born from the union of Shu and Tefnut. Initially, Nut and her brother Geb (earth) were locked in a perpetual embrace, preventing anything from existing between them. This angered Ra, the sun god, who ordered Shu to separate them. Shu, with great effort, lifted Nut high above Geb, creating the space for the world to exist. This separation became a fundamental aspect of the Egyptian understanding of the cosmos, with Nut forming the celestial canopy above the earth.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the most prominent myths involving Nut concerns her relationship with Ra. Ra decreed that Nut could not give birth on any day of the year. However, Nut sought the aid of Thoth, the god of wisdom, who gambled with the moon for extra light. Thoth won enough moonlight to create five extra days, which were added to the Egyptian calendar. On each of these five days, Nut gave birth to one of her children: Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and in some accounts, Horus the Elder. Another key myth is Nut's role in the daily cycle of the sun. She swallows Ra each evening, causing the sunset, and then gives birth to him each morning, representing the sunrise and the cycle of rebirth. This association with the sun solidified her position as a vital cosmic deity.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While Nut did not have widespread, dedicated temples in the same way as some other major Egyptian deities, she was a significant figure in funerary beliefs and royal ideology. Her image was often depicted on the inside of sarcophagus lids, symbolizing her protective role in guiding the deceased through the afterlife. By being enclosed within Nut, the deceased would symbolically be reborn just as Ra was reborn each morning. Priests of other major deities, such as Ra and Thoth, would have incorporated hymns and prayers to Nut in their rituals, acknowledging her crucial role in the cosmic order. Her connection to the sky and the stars also imbued her with a sense of majesty and power, making her a revered figure in Egyptian religious thought.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Nut's legacy extends beyond ancient Egypt, influencing modern interpretations of mythology and cosmology. Her image as the arched sky goddess remains a powerful symbol of the cosmos and the cyclical nature of time. She is often invoked in modern pagan and Wiccan traditions as a representation of the divine feminine and the celestial realm. The concept of Nut swallowing and rebirthing the sun resonates with contemporary ecological and spiritual perspectives on renewal and transformation. Her depiction in art and literature continues to inspire awe and wonder, reminding us of the vastness and mystery of the universe and the enduring power of ancient mythology.

powers: Granting Safe Passage to the Afterlife
shortDescription: Nut is the Egyptian sky goddess, depicted as an arching figure covered in stars. She swallows the sun each night and gives birth to it each morning.
